text: Hello everyone, this is Roxanne Liu and I present a large right direct inguinal hernia. This is actually a scrotal direct inguinal hernia. And I'm presenting this case because I ended up placating the transversalus fascia, which I pretty much never do. So I thought I'd share with everyone and see what your thoughts are.

The surgeon begins reducing the direct right inguinal hernia, carefully peeling away the edge of the transversalis fascia. The hernia sac primarily consists of peritoneum and preperitoneal fat, with potential bladder involvement.

The surgeon carefully navigates around the edge of the hernia defect, reducing the hernia sac by moving laterally to identify the correct anatomical plane. The presence of preperitoneal fat complicates distinguishing between fat associated with the femoral space and that descending with the hernia.

The surgeon continues the reduction of a large right direct inguinal hernia, carefully manipulating the elongated hernia sac. The process is slow due to the sac's length, and the surgeon opts to proceed without introducing an additional instrument.

The surgeon carefully presses down with scissors, attempting to create a right angle or gently grip the tissue with the scissor tip. A small bleeder is visible, prompting the surgeon to pause and ensure no further bleeding occurs on the counter sac side.
The surgeon completes the reduction of a large right direct inguinal hernia, revealing a significant defect in the space. The hernia sac appears reduced, leaving a visible large opening.

And I've never had to go back in to remove a seroma or drain a seroma. you know, it is fairly large and, you know, then this guy, I would guess it would turn into a large mango-sized seroma sac. So I'm starting to think about, well, maybe I should try to placate the transverse salis despite The warnings out there, you know, I figure I could grab small bites of the transverse salis. I know TJ Swope does that and imprecates it so that there's less dead space.

The surgeon crosses the midline to position the mesh beyond the medial edge of the direct defect. The dissection continues laterally in the space of Bogros, combining it with the space of Retzius. The surgical field shows a significant amount of adipose tissue, and the surgeon follows a natural plane to avoid disturbing the iliac and femoral fat.

The surgeon aims to achieve at least two centimeters of depth to ensure adequate posterior mesh coverage. The dissection begins laterally over the testicular vessels and progresses medially into the pelvic region.

The surgeon encounters bowel closely adherent to the peritoneum and avoids using cautery. They attempt to plicate the transversalis fascia, discovering an additional layer upon lifting it. The surgeon incises this layer and considers using CO₂ insufflation to facilitate further reduction of the transversalis fascia. Another layer is identified, but the surgeon decides against removing the inner lining due to its complexity.

The surgeon uses a needle driver to apply a 3-0 V-Lock suture, attempting to take small bites of the transversalis fascia. The process is challenging, with difficulty in pulling the transversalis into the space while being cautious of the nearby genital branch.

The surgeon attempts to make small suturing purchases on the transverse salis, being cautious of the nerve that may be closely positioned behind it. The goal is to close the dead space without merely grabbing the rim of the defect.

The surgeon aggressively takes larger bites of the hernia sac tip, pulling the mass of transversalis into the space. The transversalis is sutured down to Cooper's ligament using a V-lock suture, which locks it in place to prevent slipping.

The surgeon takes another suture bite on Cooper's ligament to prevent the counter sack from re-entering the inguinal canal, creating a stable wall for mesh placement. The suture is locked to ensure it remains secure. The surgeon prepares to insert a 3D Max mid extra-large mesh.

The surgeon places an extra-large Three D Max mesh in the pelvic region, ensuring it conforms well to the space. The mesh is positioned to cover the direct space, extending past the midline. The surgeon adjusts the mesh laterally and rotates it clockwise for better fit, and secures it with a stitch in Cooper's ligament to prevent lifting or sliding.

Using the robotic system, the surgeon places an additional stitch to secure the mesh over a large direct defect, ensuring it does not eventrate. The mesh is positioned well laterally, with the posterior lateral edge tucked into a pocket to prevent lifting. The surgeon checks the posterior edge of the mesh, ensuring it is properly seated in the retropubic space and tucked under the hernia sac. The large hernia sac is visible, and the surgeon proceeds to close the peritoneal defect and flap using a six-inch V-lock suture.

The surgeon decreases the pneumoperitoneum to 8 mmHg to desufflate the pocket and press the peritoneum against the MaxMid mesh. The CO₂ is expelled from the preperitoneal space before closing the defect, achieving good apposition of the peritoneum against the mesh.
